subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 7. In this unit you will take your understanding of fractions to a genuinely advanced level. You already know what a fraction is and how to find simple equivalent fractions, so now we build on that foundation to master all four operations — adding, subtracting, multiplying and dividing — with fractions, mixed numbers and even negative fractions. You will also learn to find fractions of amounts, and to move confidently between fractions, decimals and percentages, which are simply three different languages for the same idea.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the standardized Cambridge Stage 8 workbook source rows for screen presentation.

outcomes: Interpret a fraction as a part of a whole, a ratio, and a division, and convert freely between improper fractions and mixed numbers.
Find equivalent fractions and simplify to lowest terms using the highest common factor.
Compare and order fractions (including negative fractions) using common denominators, cross multiplication and decimals.
Add and subtract fractions and mixed numbers with unlike denominators, including borrowing.
Multiply fractions and mixed numbers, using cross-cancelling to simplify.
Divide fractions and mixed numbers using the multiply-by-the-reciprocal method.
Apply the correct sign rules when working with negative fractions.
Find a fraction of an amount and solve reverse problems.
Convert between fractions, decimals and percentages and use these links to compare quantities.
Solve multi-step word problems and check answers for reasonableness.

concepts.title: What a Fraction Really Means
concepts.explanation: A fraction can be read in three connected ways, and a strong mathematician moves easily between them. Take 3/4. First, it is a part of a whole: 3 out of 4 equal parts, like 3 of the 4 quarters of an anjero. Second, it is a ratio: the relationship 3 to 4. Third, it is a division: 3 divided by 4, which equals 0.75. These are not three different fractions — they are three views of the same object, and being fluent in all three is what lets you attack any problem. Every fraction has a numerator (the top, counting how…
concepts.example: Why does the 'division' reading matter so much? Because it is the bridge to decimals and percentages, which you will meet in Concept 7. If a fraction is genuinely just a division waiting to happen, then 3/4 and '3…

concepts.title: Comparing and Ordering Fractions
concepts.explanation: To compare fractions you must compare like with like. There are several reliable methods, and choosing the right one saves time. Method 1 — Common denominators. Rewrite the fractions so they share a denominator, then compare numerators. To compare 2/3 and 3/4, the lowest common denominator of 3 and 4 is 12, so 2/3 = 8/12 and 3/4 = 9/12. Since 8/12 < 9/12, we have 2/3 < 3/4. This method is the most reliable, especially when ordering three or more fractions.
concepts.example: Method 2 — Cross multiplication. To compare a/b and c/d, compare the products a × d and b × c. To compare 3/5 and 4/7, work out 3 × 7 = 21 and 5 × 4 = 20. Since 21 > 20, 3/5 > 4/7. This is quick for exactly two…

concepts.title: Adding and Subtracting Fractions
concepts.explanation: You can only add or subtract fractions when they are cut into pieces of the same size — that is, when they share a denominator. With like denominators, simply add or subtract the numerators and keep the denominator: 2/7 + 3/7 = 5/7, and 5/8 − 2/8 = 3/8. Always simplify the result: 7/9 − 4/9 = 3/9 = 1/3. With unlike denominators, first find the lowest common denominator (LCD), rewrite each fraction as an equivalent fraction over that denominator, then add or subtract. For 1/4 + 1/6, the LCD of 4 and 6 is 12, so 1/4…
concepts.example: There are two good methods. Method A — parts separately: add the whole numbers, then add the fractions, then combine. For 2 1/4 + 1 5/8, the wholes give 2 + 1 = 3, and the fractions give 1/4 + 5/8 = 2/8 + 5/8 = 7/8, so…

concepts.title: Multiplying Fractions
concepts.explanation: Multiplying is the most forgiving of the four operations, because you do not need a common denominator. Simply multiply the numerators together and multiply the denominators together: a/b × c/d = (a × c)/(b × d). For example, 2/3 × 1/4 = 2/12 = 1/6, and 3/5 × 2/7 = 6/35. Conceptually, multiplying fractions means taking a 'fraction of a fraction'. The calculation 1/2 × 3/4 means 'one half of three-quarters'. Start with 3/4 and take half of it, and you land on 3/8. This is why multiplying by a proper fraction (less…
concepts.example: The opposite also holds, and it is worth pausing on. Multiplying by an improper fraction (greater than 1) makes a number larger, and multiplying by exactly 1 — in any disguise, such as 5/5 or 8/8 — leaves it unchanged.…

concepts.title: Dividing Fractions
concepts.explanation: Why does flipping work? Division asks 'how many of these fit into that?'. The calculation 3/4 ÷ 1/8 means 'how many eighths are in three-quarters?'. Since 3/4 = 6/8, the answer is 6. Using the rule: 3/4 × 8/1 = 24/4 = 6. The two agree, which is exactly what we should expect. For 2/3 ÷ 4/5: keep 2/3, change to ×, flip 4/5 to 5/4, then 2/3 × 5/4 = 10/12 = 5/6. For a whole number, 8 ÷ 2/3 = 8/1 × 3/2 = 24/2 = 12 — there are twelve two-thirds in 8. Notice that dividing by a fraction less than 1 makes the answer…
concepts.example: Convert to improper fractions first. For 3 1/4 ÷ 1 2/3, convert to 13/4 ÷ 5/3 = 13/4 × 3/5 = 39/20 = 1 19/20. The sign rules for division are identical to those for multiplication, so 3/4 ÷ (−1/2) = 3/4 × (−2/1) = −6/4…

concepts.title: Fractions of an Amount
concepts.explanation: Finding a fraction of an amount is a multiplication in disguise, and it appears constantly in real life. To find 3/5 of 40, either multiply directly — 3/5 × 40 = 120/5 = 24 — or use the two-step rule that many people find quicker: divide by the denominator, then multiply by the numerator. So 40 ÷ 5 = 8 (that is one-fifth), and 8 × 3 = 24 (that is three-fifths). Both give 24. For example, Yusuf earns 3500 shillings helping at the market and gives 2/7 of it to help a neighbour. One-seventh is 3500 ÷ 7 = 500, so…
concepts.example: Finding a fraction of an amount is a multiplication in disguise, and it appears constantly in real life. To find 3/5 of 40, either multiply directly — 3/5 × 40 = 120/5 = 24 — or use the two-step rule that many people…

methods.title: Fraction of an amount
steps: Divide the amount by the denominator to find one part.
Multiply that result by the numerator.
To find the whole from a part, divide by the numerator, then multiply by the denominator.

methods.title: Converting between forms
steps: Fraction to decimal: divide numerator by denominator.
Decimal to percentage: multiply by 100. Percentage to decimal: divide by 100.
Fraction to percentage: convert to a decimal, then multiply by 100.

methods.title: Simplifying a fraction
methods.example: A plank is 8 5/8 inches long. Hassan cuts off 2 3/4 inches. What length remains?
steps: Find the highest common factor (HCF) of numerator and denominator.
Divide both by the HCF.
Check nothing but 1 divides both numbers now.

methods.title: Multiply with cross-cancelling
steps: Solution: Cancel 5 with 15 (both ÷ 5) and 4 with 8 (both ÷ 4): this gives 1/2 × 1/3 = 1/6.
Cross-cancelling meant no large numbers and no simplifying at the end.
Check the multiply with cross-cancelling result carefully.

methods.title: Divide fractions
methods.example: Amina has 6 cups of flour and each batch of anjero needs 3/4 cup. How many batches can she make?
steps: Solution: This asks how many 3/4 fit into 6, so 6 ÷ 3/4 = 6/1 × 4/3 = 24/3 = 8 batches.
Check the divide fractions result carefully.
Check the divide fractions result carefully.

methods.title: Fraction of an amount and its reverse
methods.example: (a) Find 5/9 of 63 goats. (b) If 4/7 of a flock is 28 sheep, how big is the whole flock?
steps: Solution: (a) 63 ÷ 9 = 7, then 7 × 5 = 35 goats.
(b) One-seventh is 28 ÷ 4 = 7, so the whole flock is 7 × 7 = 49 sheep.
Check the fraction of an amount and its reverse result carefully.

workedExamples.title: Simplify a fraction
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The HCF of 42 and 56 is 14. Dividing both by 14 gives 42 ÷ 14 = 3 and 56 ÷ 14 = 4, so 42/56 = 3/4.

workedExamples.title: Order fractions
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The LCM of 6, 4 and 9 is 36. Convert: 5/6 = 30/36, 3/4 = 27/36, 7/9 = 28/36. Reading numerators 27, 28, 30 gives 3/4 < 7/9 < 5/6.

workedExamples.title: Subtract mixed numbers with borrowing
workedExamples.prompt: A plank is 8 5/8 inches long. Hassan cuts off 2 3/4 inches. What length remains?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Write 3/4 as 6/8, so we need 8 5/8 − 2 6/8. Since 6/8 is larger than 5/8, borrow one whole: 8 5/8 = 7 13/8. Then 7 13/8 − 2 6/8 = 5 7/8. The plank has 5 7/8 inches left.

workedExamples.title: Multiply with cross-cancelling
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Cancel 5 with 15 (both ÷ 5) and 4 with 8 (both ÷ 4): this gives 1/2 × 1/3 = 1/6. Cross-cancelling meant no large numbers and no simplifying at the end.

workedExamples.title: Divide fractions
workedExamples.prompt: Amina has 6 cups of flour and each batch of anjero needs 3/4 cup. How many batches can she make?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: This asks how many 3/4 fit into 6, so 6 ÷ 3/4 = 6/1 × 4/3 = 24/3 = 8 batches.

workedExamples.title: Fraction of an amount and its reverse
workedExamples.prompt: (a) Find 5/9 of 63 goats. (b) If 4/7 of a flock is 28 sheep, how big is the whole flock?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: (a) 63 ÷ 9 = 7, then 7 × 5 = 35 goats. (b) One-seventh is 28 ÷ 4 = 7, so the whole flock is 7 × 7 = 49 sheep.

workedExamples.title: Fraction–decimal–percentage comparison
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Convert to the same form: 7/8 = 0.875 = 87.5%. Since 87.5% > 85%, 7/8 is larger.

workedExamples.title: Negative fractions in context
workedExamples.prompt: One morning the temperature is −1 3/4°C and it rises by 3 1/2°C by midday. What is the midday temperature?

workedExamples.title: Guided example 11
workedExamples.prompt: Convert to decimals and percentages: a) 3/5 b) 7/8 c) 9/20.

activities.title: The Kitchen Fraction Lab (Recipe Scaling)
activities.materials: a simple recipe (for anjero, bariis or a fruit salad), measuring cups or a clean container, and paper.
steps: Write out the ingredients of a recipe that serves 4 people.
Work out the scale factor to serve 10 people (10/4 = 5/2 = 2 1/2).
Multiply each ingredient by 2 1/2, converting any mixed numbers to improper fractions first.
If any answer is an improper fraction, convert it back to a mixed number so it is easy to measure.
Check one amount by estimating: does 'about two and a half times more' look right?

activities.title: The Measuring Tape Challenge (Adding and Subtracting)
activities.materials: a tape measure or ruler marked in fractions of an inch, and some objects to measure.
steps: Measure three objects to the nearest 1/8 inch and write each length as a mixed number.
Add two of the lengths together, finding a common denominator.
Find the difference between the longest and shortest object, borrowing if you need to.
Ask a family member to check your measurements and your arithmetic.

activities.title: Fraction War (A Comparing Game)
activities.materials: paper cards, each showing a fraction (mix easy and tricky denominators).
steps: Shuffle the cards and deal them equally between two players.
Each player turns over one card at the same time.
Whoever has the larger fraction wins both cards — use common denominators or decimals to decide.
If the fractions are equal, turn over another card to break the tie. Play until one player holds all the cards.
This game trains you to compare fractions quickly and to justify which is larger.

activities.title: The Discount Detective (Fractions, Decimals, Percentages)
activities.materials: a few price tags or made-up prices, and paper.
steps: Invent two shops selling the same item, one advertising a fraction off (say 3/8) and one a percentage off (say 35%).
Convert both discounts to percentages so they are in the same language.
Work out the final price in each shop.
Decide which shop is the better deal and explain your reasoning in one sentence.

activities.title: The Fair-Share Investigation
activities.materials: a bag of dates, beans or stones, and family members or friends to share with.
steps: Count out a number of dates that does not divide evenly among your group (for example 20 dates among 3 people).
Work out each person's share as a fraction and as a mixed number.
Now share 2/5 of the whole bag for charity: find the fraction of the amount.
Investigate: for which group sizes does the sharing come out exactly, and for which is there a fractional remainder? Record what you notice.

activities.title: Build a Fraction Wall
activities.materials: paper, a ruler, a pencil, and colours.
steps: Draw several equal-length bars stacked on top of each other.
Split the first into 1 part, the next into halves, then thirds, quarters, fifths, sixths, eighths, and so on.
Colour each row and label every piece with its fraction.
Use your wall to spot equivalent fractions (which pieces line up exactly?) and to compare sizes at a glance.
Keep your fraction wall — it is a powerful visual aid for the whole unit.

rules.title: Key idea 1
rules.text: Why does the 'division' reading matter so much? Because it is the bridge to decimals and percentages, which you will meet in Concept 7. If a fraction is genuinely just a division waiting to happen, then 3/4 and '3 divided by 4' and 0.75 are all the same number wearing different clothes. Keeping this in mind stops fractions from feeling like a separate, mysterious topic — they are simply numbers, and every rule you already know about numbers still applies to them.

rules.title: Key idea 2
rules.text: Why does flipping work? Division asks 'how many of these fit into that?'. The calculation 3/4 ÷ 1/8 means 'how many eighths are in three-quarters?'. Since 3/4 = 6/8, the answer is 6. Using the rule: 3/4 × 8/1 = 24/4 = 6. The two agree, which is exactly what we should expect.

rules.title: Key idea 3
rules.text: Finding a fraction of an amount is a multiplication in disguise, and it appears constantly in real life. To find 3/5 of 40, either multiply directly — 3/5 × 40 = 120/5 = 24 — or use the two-step rule that many people find quicker: divide by the denominator, then multiply by the numerator. So 40 ÷ 5 = 8 (that is one-fifth), and 8 × 3 = 24 (that is three-fifths). Both give 24.

terms: Top number / bottom number of a fraction
Value less than 1 / value 1 or more
A whole number plus a proper fraction
Different fractions with the same value
Highest common factor / lowest common denominator
commonMistakes: Find the LCD; add only the numerators
Multiply straight across; no LCD needed
Change to an improper fraction first
Keep the first; flip only the second
Larger distance from zero = smaller value

realProblems.prompt: At the Kismaayo market, Sahra buys 3 3/4 kg of rice and uses 2 1/2 kg for a family meal. How much rice is left?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A plank is 6 metres long. Liban needs pieces that are 3/8 metre each. How many complete pieces can he cut, and how much is wasted?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A charity shares 5/6 of a 4200-shilling collection among families and keeps the rest. How much is shared, and how much is kept?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A recipe for 6 people needs 3 1/3 cups of flour. Rewrite the amount needed for 15 people.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: One shop offers 3/8 off a 400-shilling item and another offers 40% off. Work out both final prices and say which is cheaper.
realProblems.answer: shop A: 3/8 of 400 = 150 off, price 250; shop B: 40% of 400 = 160 off, price 240; shop B is cheaper.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: shop A: 3/8 of 400 = 150 off, price 250; shop B: 40% of 400 = 160 off, price 240; shop B is cheaper.

realProblems.prompt: A journey from Nairobi to Mombasa is 4/5 complete after 384 km. How long is the whole journey?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Work out 5 1/6 − 2 3/4, showing your borrowing clearly.
Work out 2 2/5 × 1 7/8, giving your answer as a mixed number.
Work out 3 1/3 ÷ 1 1/9, giving your answer as a mixed number.
Which is largest: 5/8, 0.62, or 61%? Show your comparison.
If 5/12 of a delivery of mangoes is 60 mangoes, how many mangoes are in the whole delivery?

questions.question: What does “Numerator / Denominator” mean?
options: Top number / bottom number of a fraction
Value less than 1 / value 1 or more
A whole number plus a proper fraction
Different fractions with the same value
questions.answer: Top number / bottom number of a fraction
questions.hint: Use the Unit 7 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Numerator / Denominator means Top number / bottom number of a fraction.

selfAssessment: I can interpret a fraction as a part of a whole, a ratio, and a division, and convert freely between improper fractions and mixed numbers.
I can find equivalent fractions and simplify to lowest terms using the highest common factor.
I can compare and order fractions (including negative fractions) using common denominators, cross multiplication and decimals.
I can add and subtract fractions and mixed numbers with unlike denominators, including borrowing.
I can multiply fractions and mixed numbers, using cross-cancelling to simplify.
I can divide fractions and mixed numbers using the multiply-by-the-reciprocal method.
I can apply the correct sign rules when working with negative fractions.
I can find a fraction of an amount and solve reverse problems.
